skill.name: add-malli-schemas
skill.description: Efficiently add Malli schemas to Metabase API endpoints with consistent parameter validation, response schemas, and error handling.
skill.summary: Add consistent Malli request and response schemas to Metabase Clojure API endpoints.
skill.author: metabase

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are almost entirely Markdown code examples, inline file paths, and schema terminology rather than executable skill code. The only confirmed risk factors are a user-initiated local REPL command and one external documentation link; no credential access, prompt injection, malicious network behavior, or obfuscated payload was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Examples Flagged as Code Execution
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick findings are Markdown inline code and fenced Clojure examples, not executable skill implementation. Examples include reference file paths and schema syntax used to guide Metabase API edits.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is a Markdown instruction document, and the cited content is displayed as examples. There is no script, hook, or executable wrapper that would run these snippets automatically.

low_findings.title: False Positive: API Key Path Is Not Secret Access
low_findings.description: The env_access finding points to a repository path containing the text api_keys. It does not read environment variables, tokens, credentials, or secret files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 13 is an inline Markdown path to a Metabase API keys namespace. No environment variable access or credential extraction appears in the skill.

low_findings.title: Benign Local REPL Command Guidance
low_findings.description: The skill suggests a local Metabase mage REPL command for inspecting a Clojure docstring while developing schemas. This is user-initiated project tooling, but it should still be reviewed before execution in an untrusted checkout.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: There is a real external command instruction, but it invokes local project tooling with a fixed expression. No user-controlled shell interpolation or remote execution behavior is present.

low_findings.title: Benign External Documentation Link
low_findings.description: The network finding is a Markdown link to public Malli documentation. It is not an automatic request and does not transmit local data or credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded URL is present, but it is only a documentation reference. The skill contains no fetch, curl, webhook, or other network execution mechanism.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Critical and Crypto Keywords Are Documentation Text
low_findings.description: The blocker and obfuscation findings are caused by schema documentation words, bracket-heavy Clojure examples, and emphasis text such as critical concept. No weak cryptographic implementation, obfuscated payload, or dangerous execution chain was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Semantic review shows the cited material is YAML front matter and explanatory schema validation guidance. It does not contain encryption code, packed JavaScript, or instructions to bypass review.

content.user_title: Add Malli Schemas to Metabase APIs
content.value_statement: Metabase API endpoints need consistent schemas for parameters, request bodies, and responses. This skill gives practical patterns for adding Malli schemas that match Metabase validation timing and coding conventions.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to add route, query, body, and response schemas to Metabase endpoints.
Explains when to use Metabase Malli utility schemas such as positive integers and boolean values.
Documents response validation timing before JSON serialization for Java Time objects, sets, and keywords.
Provides examples for inline schemas, named reusable schemas, enum schemas, and paginated responses.
Lists common schema mistakes and corrected patterns for optional, nullable, and defaulted fields.
Suggests a testing workflow for valid requests, invalid requests, optional parameters, and error messages.
limitations: It is tailored to the Metabase Clojure codebase and its API macros.
It does not automatically inspect endpoint return values or generate schemas from running code.
It assumes the developer can read nearby Clojure implementations and tests.
It gives guidance only; final schema correctness still requires local tests and code review.

use_cases.title: Add Schemas to a New Endpoint
use_cases.description: Use the checklist and examples to add parameter and response schemas while creating a Metabase API endpoint.

use_cases.title: Modernize Existing API Handlers
use_cases.description: Apply named schemas, optional parameter rules, and response validation guidance to older endpoints.

use_cases.title: Review Schema Changes
use_cases.description: Check pull requests for validation timing errors, nullable fields, defaults, and reusable schema opportunities.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Basic Route Schema
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner task for a simple endpoint with one route parameter.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add a Malli schema to the route parameter for this Metabase endpoint. Keep the change minimal and explain the chosen schema type.

prompt_templates.title: Add Query and Body Schemas
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate task for request validation with optional values.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add Malli schemas for the query parameters and request body in this endpoint. Preserve defaults and mark optional fields correctly.

prompt_templates.title: Define a Response Schema
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate task for validating returned Clojure data.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to inspect the endpoint return shape and add a response schema. Account for validation before JSON serialization.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Shared API Schemas
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced task for repeated or nested response structures.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to replace repeated inline schemas with named Malli schemas. Keep the names clear and update all related endpoints consistently.

output_examples.input: A developer asks for schema coverage on an endpoint with route parameters and an optional include query parameter.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies route and query inputs, recommends required and optional schema fields, and describes the validation behavior to test.

output_examples.input: A response schema fails because a date field is not a string during validation.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains that response validation runs before JSON serialization and recommends accepting the internal Java Time value.

output_examples.input: A pull request repeats the same nested user shape in several endpoints.
output_examples.output: The assistant suggests a named reusable schema and describes where to apply it without changing endpoint behavior.

best_practices: Prefer existing Metabase Malli utility schemas before introducing new predicate-based schemas.
Separate route, query, and body schemas so validation matches the endpoint contract.
Test both valid and invalid requests after adding response and parameter schemas.
anti_patterns: Do not use request-time string schemas for response values that are still Java or Clojure objects.
Do not make optional query parameters required unless the endpoint contract requires them.
Do not duplicate complex inline schemas when a named reusable schema would be clearer.

faq.question: What codebase is this skill designed for?
faq.answer: It is designed for Metabase API endpoints written in Clojure with Metabase API macros and Malli schemas.

faq.question: Does this skill generate schemas automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and review guidance. The developer still needs to inspect endpoint behavior and tests.

faq.question: Can it help with response schemas?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains inline and named response schemas, nested response shapes, paginated responses, and validation timing.

faq.question: Why does validation timing matter?
faq.answer: Response schemas validate Clojure data before JSON serialization, so some fields are not strings yet.

faq.question: Should optional query parameters use defaults?
faq.answer: Use defaults when the endpoint already has a defined default behavior. Otherwise mark optional fields without changing semantics.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. Review found no malicious behavior. The only confirmed risks are a local command example and an external documentation link.
